coredata.dc:title: Influence of surface structure and tube material on the condensation heat transfer coefficient of propane on horizontal single tubes and in tube bundles
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Kühl, Julius V.W.
Jander, Julius H.
Piszko, Maximilian
Freitag, Detlef
Dietl, Jochen
El-Hajal, Jean
Gotterbarm, Achim
Rausch, Michael H.
Klein, Tobias
Fröba, Andreas P.
coredata.dc:description: The condensation heat transfer of propane on the outside of single horizontal tubes and in tube bundles was investigated by determining the condensation heat transfer coefficient α cond in a newly designed experimental setup. To study the influence of different surface structures and tube materials, experiments were conducted with a smooth tube, finned tubes with various fin densities and heights, and a high-performance condensation tube at vapor temperatures of 30 and 40 °C and varying heat flux densities. In addition, the influence of inundation on α cond was systematically investigated by varying the mass flow of liquid condensate at heat flux densities of 20 and 60 kW·m− 2. The experimental setup was successfully validated using smooth tubes by a comparison of the resulting α cond with literature data and Nußelt's film theory. For the single copper tubes with fin densities from 19 to 48 fins per inch, α cond increased and was by factors of 9 to 21 larger than for the smooth tube at the same heat flux density. Investigations of the inundation effect have shown that the additional condensate can lead to an increasing condensate film thickness, which decreases α cond due to its increased thermal resistance, and/or can lead to turbulences in the condensate film, which increases α cond. The interplay of the two effects depends highly on the surface geometry. An increasing fin height always enhanced α cond, whereas a lower thermal conductivity of the tube material leads to a smaller α cond.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Propane
Condensation heat transfer
Finned tubes
Single tube
Tube bundle

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Modelling of the condensation heat transfer coefficient on the outside of horizontal finned tubes
Validation of the experimental setup with smooth copper tubes
Influences of fin density, fin height, and tube material

The authors gratefully acknowledge funding of the Erlangen Graduate School in Advanced Optical Technologies (SAOT) by the Bavarian State Ministry for Science and Art.

This work was financially supported by the Bayerische Forschungsstiftung via the project Grant AZ-1485-20. The authors gratefully acknowledge funding of the Erlangen Graduate School in Advanced Optical Technologies (SAOT) by the Bavarian State Ministry for Science and Art.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the experimental apparatus for the investigation of the condensation heat transfer coefficient. The flow paths of the working fluid propane between the evaporator and the condenser (dashed lines) and of the liquid condensate for the investigation of the inundation effect (solid lines) are given. Additionally, a cross-section of the condenser is included where the perforated tubes for the investigation of the inundation effect (1) and the four rows of condensation tubes (2–5) are highlighted.
Inundation experiment for a finned copper-nickel tube with a fin density of 30 fpi at ϑ sat = 30 °C, q̇ = 20.6 kW·m− 2, and Re film = 442.4.
Picture of the installed experimental setup before applying the thermal insulation.
Schematic representation of the finned (left) and high-performance (right) condensation tubes.

Condensation heat transfer coefficient α cond from experiments (symbols) and theory based on Nußelt [9] (lines) for the condensation of propane on a smooth copper tube as function of the heat flux density q̇.

Condensation of propane on a smooth copper tube at heat flux densities of 6 kW·m− 2 (left) or 20 kW·m− 2 (right). In both cases, condensation took place only on the topmost condensation tube row while the other three rows were inactive.

Condensation heat transfer coefficient α cond for the condensation of propane on finned copper tubes with different fin density and a fin height of 0.9 mm as well as a high-performance condensation tube (HPT) as function of the heat flux density q̇ at ϑ sat = 40 °C. The expanded experimental uncertainties (k = 2) are shown only for the 19 and 48 fpi tubes and for the HPT to ensure legibility.

Condensation heat transfer coefficients α cond for the condensation of propane on finned tubes with 30 fpi and the different tube materials copper, Cu, and a copper-nickel alloy, CuNi, as well as different fin heights as function of the heat flux density q̇ at ϑ sat = 40 °C. The expanded experimental uncertainties (k = 2) are shown only for the Cu tube with 0.9 mm fin height and the CuNi tube with 0.6 mm fin height to ensure legibility.

Condensation heat transfer coefficient calculated by the model of Al-Badri et al. [6,16], α cond,pred, and from the experiments in this work, α cond,exp, for the finned condensation tubes with a variation in fin density, fin height, and tube material at ϑ sat = 30 and 40 °C. The dashed lines represent deviations of 15 %.

Condensation of propane on the 30 fpi Cu tube at a heat flux density of 20 kW·m− 2 and ϑ sat = 30 °C without additional irrigation (left) and with additional irrigation according to Re film = 1529 (right).

Condensation heat transfer coefficients α cond during condensate irrigation related to the respective value for the single tube α ref for the condensation of propane on different tubes as function of the film Reynolds number Re film at a heat flux density q̇ of approximately 20 kW·m− 2 and ϑ sat = 40 °C. The expanded experimental uncertainties (k = 2) are shown only for the smooth tube and the 40 fpi tube to ensure legibility.
Investigated condensation tubes with variation in the tube material, fin density, fin height, and the fin root diameter d outside, which was used for the calculation of A outside.

entry.bold: fin root diameter d out side / mm

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Condensation heat transfer coefficient α cond as function of the heat flux density q̇ at a given temperature of the saturated vapor ϑ sat for the different investigated single tubes. Additionally, the relative expanded experimental uncertainty U r(α cond) (k = 2) is given.
a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The uncertainty of ϑ sat calculated from measured p sat is estimated to U(ϑ sat) = 0.020 K.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Condensation heat transfer coefficient α cond as function of the film Reynolds number Re film and heat flux density q̇ at a given temperature of the saturated vapor ϑ sat = 40 °C during the inundation experiments.
a
Additionally, the relative expanded experimental uncertainty U r(α cond) (k = 2) is given.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The uncertainty of ϑ sat calculated from measured p sat is estimated to U(ϑ sat) = 0.020 K.

head.ce:title: Influence of surface structure and tube material on the condensation heat transfer coefficient of propane on horizontal single tubes and in tube bundles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The condensation heat transfer of propane on the outside of single horizontal tubes and in tube bundles was investigated by determining the condensation heat transfer coefficient α cond in a newly designed experimental setup. To study the influence of different surface structures and tube materials, experiments were conducted with a smooth tube, finned tubes with various fin densities and heights, and a high-performance condensation tube at vapor temperatures of 30 and 40 °C and varying heat flux densities. In addition, the influence of inundation on α cond was systematically investigated by varying the mass flow of liquid condensate at heat flux densities of 20 and 60 kW·m− 2. The experimental setup was successfully validated using smooth tubes by a comparison of the resulting α cond with literature data and Nußelt's film theory. For the single copper tubes with fin densities from 19 to 48 fins per inch, α cond increased and was by factors of 9 to 21 larger than for the smooth tube at the same heat flux density. Investigations of the inundation effect have shown that the additional condensate can lead to an increasing condensate film thickness, which decreases α cond due to its increased thermal resistance, and/or can lead to turbulences in the condensate film, which increases α cond. The interplay of the two effects depends highly on the surface geometry. An increasing fin height always enhanced α cond, whereas a lower thermal conductivity of the tube material leads to a smaller α cond.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The primary measurement data are included as Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:para: For mitigating global warming, great efforts are spent to replace so-called greenhouse gases like fluorinated hydrocarbons, which are still used as working fluids in refrigeration plants and heat pumps. Such fluids like 1,1,1,2-tetrafluoroethane (R134a) are very effective greenhouse gases and exhibit high global warming potential (GWP) [1]. Although the overall contribution of these gases to global warming is rather small because of the smaller amount emitted in comparison to CO2, increasing global temperatures and population lead to an increased need for air conditioning and refrigeration systems and, thus, alternative refrigerants [2,3]. Possible alternatives, e.g., for R134a with a GWP of 1300, are natural refrigerants such as propane (R290) and n-butane (R600) with GWPs of 3 and 4, respectively [4]. Additionally, the amount of working fluid needed can often be reduced by designing more compact and efficient heat exchangers. For shell-and-tube heat exchangers, which are used in various plants in chemical and energy technology, a significant improvement of the heat transfer can be achieved by using tubes with enhanced surfaces such as finned tubes [5]. Especially in the case of condensation, the use of finned tubes has shown to greatly improve the heat transfer [6–8]. In contrast to smooth tubes, where the condensation heat transfer coefficient α cond between the tube wall and the saturated vapor can be described well by Nußelt's film theory [9], theoretical calculations of α cond on finned tubes are still challenging and often show large deviations from experimental data [6,10,11]. For the establishment of models and the efficient design of heat exchangers, experimental data for α cond at process-relevant conditions are required. While such data exist in the literature for fluorinated hydrocarbons [12–15], they are still scarce for natural refrigerants. Here, investigations of α cond for finned tubes with a systematic variation of parameters such as the tube material or the fin density and fin height are required for the optimal design of condensation tubes and corresponding heat exchangers. Since these are typically designed as tube-bundle heat exchangers, also the effect of inundation, i.e. of liquid condensate dripping on lower tube rows, must be studied.

For the aforementioned reasons, this work presents experimental and theoretical results for the heat transfer coefficient during the condensation of pure propane on the outside of single tubes and in tube bundles at saturated vapor temperatures of ϑ sat = 30 and 40 °C. For this, a new experimental apparatus similar to that described in the work of Gebauer et al. [7] was built up and validated by comparing α cond measured for propane on smooth copper tubes with values from the literature and Nußelt's film theory [9]. The influence of the tube surface geometry on α cond is investigated by a systematic variation of the fin density and fin height as well as by a high-performance heat transfer tube. Furthermore, the comparison between α cond for geometrically identical tubes made of copper and a copper-nickel alloy allows to study the influence of the tube material. To investigate the influence of inundation on α cond, the tube bundle effect is simulated by irrigating liquid condensate on selected finned tubes and the high-performance heat transfer tube during the condensation process. The experimental investigations for the finned tubes are compared with theoretical calculations using the model of Al-Badri et al. [6,16].

Experiments were performed with the newly constructed condensation apparatus which is based on the design of Gebauer et al. [7] and which will be briefly presented in the following. A simplified scheme of the setup is shown in Fig. 1 .

The main components of the setup are the evaporator and the condenser which are both equipped with horizontally arranged tubes with an outer diameter of 19 mm. The evaporation and the condensation of the working fluid propane is realized with the help of preconditioned water flows. The condenser tubes have an effective length of 1.46 m and are arranged in four rows of two tubes each, c.f. Fig. 1. The cooling water flows through both tubes in each row consecutively, extending the effective length for the condensation tubes per row to 2.92 m. For the determination of α cond for a single tube, only one of the four condensation tube rows were supplied with cooling water, while all other condensation tube rows were kept at a slightly higher ϑ than the saturated vapor. To determine the heat flow into the cooling water, which is required for the determination of α cond, the volume flow rate through each condensation tube row and the temperature of the cooling water at the inlet (3) and outlet (4) are measured. Propane vapor is produced in the evaporator by supplying heat with the help of pre-conditioned water (5 and 6) and guided into the top of the condenser by three lines (2) that include a metal mesh for removing any liquid droplets (1). The liquid condensate is returned to the bottom of the evaporator either directly or, in the case of inundation experiments, indirectly via the reservoir, creating a natural circulation. A coaxial heat exchanger is connected to the condenser at the very top of the setup, c.f. (8) in Fig. 1, for removing any non-condensable gaseous impurities from the working fluid propane to avoid any influences on α cond [17–19]. For this purpose, it is operated as condenser after filling new working fluid or after exchanging condensation tubes, where non-condensable gases in the vapor stream can accumulate in the heat exchanger and be released after a given time period.

For the investigation of the inundation effect, liquid condensate is taken from the reservoir and led with the help of a centrifugal pump (7) to the condenser, where a set of perforated stainless-steel tubes is included, c.f. the cross-section of the condenser in Fig. 1. All inundation experiments were performed on the first row of condensation tubes, c.f. row number 2 in the condenser cross section in Fig. 1. This is due to the fact that only for the condensation tube row directly beneath the perforated stainless-steel tubes, a well-defined and homogenous inundation mass flow can be adjusted. The temperature of the inundation mass flow was measured before entering the perforated tubes in the condenser and adjusted to the temperature of the saturated vapor with the help of an additional plate heat exchanger. With this, a subcooling of the liquid condensate, which strongly influences α cond during inundation experiments, could be excluded. For the inundation experiments, the mass flow of the liquid condensate can be adjusted, so that the conditions in lower rows of shell-and-tube condensers can be simulated. In this experimental setup, a mass flow rate that is approximately 40 times larger than the liquid condensate mass flow rate which is condensing on a single tube can be adjusted. Therefore, tube bundles with a number of rows on the order of 40 can be simulated.

Fig. 2 shows this configuration for an inundation experiment on a finned copper nickel tube with a fin density of 30 fins per inch (fpi) at a vapor temperature of ϑ sat = 30 °C, a heat flux density of q̇ = 20.6 kW·m− 2, and an inundation mass flow corresponding to a film Reynolds number Re film = 442.4.

For monitoring the measurements conditions, 27 temperature probes, 5 pressure sensors, and 6 flow meters are included in the setup. 4 temperature probes are included in both the evaporator and condenser to access the temperature in different parts of the apparatuses. The positions of the temperature probes are indicated in Fig. 1. During the experiments, the difference between the different temperature probes in one apparatus was typically in the range of the combined uncertainty of 40 mK and in some cases up to 60 mK. For the accurate determination of temperatures, Pt 100 Ω resistance probes were calibrated with the help of a Pt 25 Ω reference thermometer, which was calibrated according to the ITS-90 with an expanded uncertainty (coverage factor k = 2) of 3.0 mK in the temperature T range relevant in the work. The expanded experimental uncertainty (k = 2) for T in this work were conservatively estimated to be 20 mK based on the uncertainty of the Pt 25 Ω reference thermometer and the uncertainties arising from the calibration procedure. The pressure p in the condenser and evaporator are recorded electronically with the help of an inline pressure transmitter with a specified measurement uncertainty U(p) = 1.4 kPa. For the determination of the volume flow rates of the heating and cooling water, magnetic-inductive flow meters with a specified measurement uncertainty of 0.3 % were used. The mass flow rate of the liquid condensate used for the inundation experiments was determined with a Coriolis flow meter with an uncertainty of 0.05 %.

To give an impression for the dimensions of the final experimental setup, a picture, taken before the experimental setup was covered by insulation to reduce heat transfer with the surrounding, is shown in Fig. 3 .

In a first step toward the calculation of α cond, the heat flow from the vapor phase to the cooling water inside the tube is accessible by calculating the increase of the enthalpy flow rate of the cooling water from the inlet to the outlet of the condensation tubes. Assuming that the cooling water can be treated as an incompressible fluid, the heat flow rate Q̇ is
(1)
Q ˙

m ˙

cw , in
)
.
Here, ṁ cw and c p,cw stand for the mass flow rate and the isobaric heat capacity of the cooling water. The corresponding values for c p,cw at the temperatures at the cooling water inlet, ϑ cw,in, and at its outlet, ϑ cw,out, were taken from the REFPROP database [20] relying on the equation of state of Wagner and Pruß [21]. Q̇ can also be expressed via the overall heat transfer coefficient k or the overall heat transfer resistance R th,total according to
(2)
Q ˙
= k ·

with the outer tube surface area A out and the logarithmic mean temperature difference (lmtd) between the saturated vapor, ϑ sat, and the cooling-water side,

.
Since the direct measurement of ϑ sat in the condenser might be influenced by the condensation of vapor on the metal mantles of the temperature probes, ϑ sat was determined from the measured saturation pressure p sat using the equation of state of Lemmon et al. [22] as implemented in the REFPROP database [20]. In general, determining ϑ sat from measured p sat yields larger α cond in comparison to the direct measurement of ϑ sat. The difference between α cond based on the two different methods is, however, always within combined uncertainties. Thus, R th,total can be determined from Eq. (2) and rewritten as the sum of thermal resistances related to the heat transfer steps from the saturated vapor to the cooling water,
(4)
R
th , total
=
1
k
A outside
=
1
α cond
A outside
+
s t
λ t
A lm
+
1
α cw
A inside
.
The outer resistance from the saturated vapor to the outside of the tube wall includes the desired α cond as well as the surface area of the outside of the tube A outside. Here, A outside is calculated on the basis of the radius at the root of the fins R out, which is given in Table 1 . α cond can then be determined by further considering the conductive heat transfer resistance through the tube wall and the convective heat transfer resistance between the inner tube wall and the cooling water. The calculation of these resistances requires the tube wall thickness s t, the thermal conductivity λ t of the tube material, the logarithmic mean surface area of the tube A lm,
(5)
A lm
=
2 π l
(
r outside
−
r inside
)
ln
(
r outside
r inside
)
,
the inner tube surface area A inside, and the inner convective heat transfer coefficient α cw. For smooth tubes, α cw was calculated using the correlation according to Gnielinski [23],
(6)
α cw
=
λ cw
d inside
( ξ / 8 )
(
R
e cw
− 1000
)
P
r cw
1 + 12.7
ξ / 8
( P
r
cw
2 / 3
− 1 )
[
1 +
(
d inside
/
l t
)
2 / 3
]
.
Here, λ cw, Re cw, and Pr cw represent the thermal conductivity, the Reynolds number and the Prandtl number of the cooling water. λ cw and the required thermophysical properties for the calculation of Re cw and Pr cw for the cooling water were taken at the average ϑ between the inlet and outlet from the REFPROP database [20], which includes reference correlations for λ and the viscosity by Huber et al. [24,25] and the equation of state by Wagner and Pruß [21] for water. In Eq. (6), d in side is the inner tube diameter and l t the effective tube length. The expression ξ indicates the friction factor, which can be calculated using the Konakov [26] expression only via the Reynolds number. All measurements in this work were performed in the ranges of 104 ≤ Re ≤ 106, 0.1 ≤ Pr ≤ 103, and 0 ≤ d inside/l t ≤ 1 in which the Gnielinski [23] equation is valid. For the finned condensation tubes, which also have a finned structure on the inner wall, own correlations for α cw as a function of Re cw and Pr cw were regressed from measured data and used. The employed values for α cw and A inside for the calculation of α cond are summarized with the measured primary data in the Supporting Information.

In the experiment, all directly measurement data in terms of temperatures, pressures and flow rates can be acquired within approximately 13 s. After initial equilibration at a given set of boundary conditions, corresponding measurements were performed over a time interval of about 25 min in which at least 95 individual readings of the measured data and, therefore, 95 individual data for α cond could be recorded. The reported α cond represent the average over all measured data for the given experiments. The associated uncertainties were estimated on a confidence level of more than 95 % (k = 2) and calculated using an error propagation in quadrature, which includes the uncertainties of the sensors, the stability of the measured quantities as well as the uncertainty specified for the employed properties. The uncertainties for α cw are estimated to be 5 % (k = 2).

The relative uncertainties for α cond depend on the boundary conditions of the investigated state points and typically vary between 5 and 15 %. The uncertainties are usually higher at low q̇, since the smaller change in the cooling water temperature leads to larger uncertainties in Q̇ and Δϑ lmtd, c.f. Eqs. (1) and (3). After a measurement series for a given single tube was finalized, repetition measurements were performed at minimum of two different q̇ to ensure the repeatability of the measured α cond. In all cases, agreement within the experimental uncertainties was found. Additionally, selected single tubes were investigated at different condenser rows to ensure that the results are independent of the position in the condenser and the corresponding cooling water configuration.

In order to quantify the measurement conditions during the inundation experiments, the film Reynolds number
(7)
R
e film
=
2 ·
m ˙
cond
l t
·
η cond
=
2 ·
(
Q ˙
Δ
h v
+
m ˙
irr
)
l t
·
η cond
is calculated by using the mass flow rate of liquid condensate ṁ cond, the tube length l t, and the dynamic viscosity of the condensate η cond. Here, ṁ cond is determined by the measured mass flow of irrigated condensate plus the condensate mass flow formed in the condensation process, as shown in Eq. (7). η cond is taken from Vogel and Herrmann [27] as included in the REFPROP database [20]. Also in the case of the tube bundle experiments, repetition measurements at different Re film were conducted after a measurement series was completed. In all cases, the results for α cond could be reproduced within the experimental uncertainties.

Propane (R290) used as refrigerant in the present study was supplied by Westfalen AG and has a specified purity of 99.5 %. The following substances in the given fractions are contained as further components/impurities: 0.5 wt.-% organic substances, 25 wt.-ppm H2O, 50 wt.-ppm high-boiling residues, and 1.5 vol.-% non-condensable gases.

Prior to filling the experimental setup with the refrigerant, the entire setup was flushed with nitrogen (N2), which was then removed from the setup with the help of a vacuum pump. Afterwards, the setup was filled with gaseous propane up to a pressure of 0.2 MPa before the setup was evacuated again. The latter process was repeated three further times to minimize the amount of non-condensable gas in the setup. The same procedure was also applied every time the condenser had to be opened for exchanging condensation tubes. Afterwards, the amount of impurities was further reduced with the help of the coaxial heat exchanger.

The condensation tubes investigated in this work were all provided by Wieland-Werke AG. They were selected to systematically study the influence of different tube characteristics on α cond. A schematic representation of the finned and high-performance condensation tubes is given in Fig. 4 .

First, a smooth deoxidized copper tube (smooth Cu) was investigated to validate the newly constructed experimental setup and data evaluation. To study the influence of surface modifications in the form of fins, different finned tubes with varying fin density from (19 to 48) fpi and a high-performance condensation tube (HPT) with a more complex fin structure were chosen. The influence of the tube material was studied by comparing α cond for condensation tubes with 30 fpi made of deoxidized copper (Cu) as well as of the copper-nickel alloy CuNi10Fe1Mn, hereafter abbreviated as CuNi. Finally, the impact of the fin height was determined by comparing 30 fpi tubes made of Cu or CuNi with fin heights of (0.9 and 0.6) mm. All tubes have a wall thickness of about 1 mm. The thickness of the fins s fin is typically 0.35 mm and the flank angle Θflank is typically 7°. The radius at the fin root R root and at the fin tips R out as well as the fin pitch s pitch can be calculated on the basis of the data given in Table 1. An overview over the investigated condensation tubes in this work is given in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modelling of the condensation heat transfer coefficient on the outside of horizontal finned tubes
ce:section.ce:para: Besides its experimental determination, also the prediction of α cond using empirical or analytic models is of interest for the design and optimization of condensation tubes and tube bundles. While a proven theoretical model for the condensation of a pure fluid on horizontal tubes already exists in the literature based on the work of Nußelt [9,26], various approaches are proposed for the prediction of α cond for finned tubes. Al-Badri et al. [6,16] developed an analytical element-by-element model for α cond and tested the predictive performance by comparison with experimental data for the condensation of propane and R134a on different finned copper tubes. Furthermore, they used the models from Beatty and Katz [28], Honda and Nozu [29], Briggs and Rose [30], and Murata and Hashizume [10] for the prediction of α cond for the same systems. The results show that the analytical model from Al-Badri et al. [6,16] is not only able to predict α cond with the smallest average absolute relative deviation (AARD) from their own experimental data, but is also able to predict the q̇-dependent trend of α cond very accurately over a wide range. For this reason, the analytical model from Al-Badri et al. [6,16] is also applied to predict α cond for the finned Cu or CuNi condensation tubes, including the variation in the fin height, for the two experimentally investigated vapor temperatures of 30 and 40 °C. In the following, the general approach of the analytical model is briefly summarized. For the complete theoretical background, the reader is referred to the original publications [6,16].

The analytical model is based on iteratively solving the temperature distribution between the cooling water and the saturated vapor. For this, the local heat transfer rate for one annular element is considered separately for the fin flank, the channel between the fins, and the fin tip. The height of the condensate in the channels between fins is determined to calculate the inactive fin height. Based on this, a mean α cond can be calculated for one complete section of the condensation tube and compared to experimental data.

The numerical results for α cond, together with ϑ sat and the expanded statistical uncertainties (k = 2), for the single tube investigations are summarized in Table 2 . Additionally, the primary experimental measured data, such as T, p, and the volume flow rates, required to calculate α cond are summarized in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the experimental setup with smooth copper tubes
ce:section.ce:para: For the validation of the experimental setup, measurements of α cond were performed for smooth copper tubes at ϑ sat = 30 and 40 °C. The results are compared with theoretical values based on the work of Nußelt [9,26] as well as with experimental data from Gebauer et al. [7], measured at ϑ sat = 37 °C as function of q̇ in Fig. 5 .

The results in Fig. 5 show that α cond decreases with increasing q̇ at a given ϑ sat. This can be explained by an increasing condensate film thickness and, thus, increasing heat transfer resistance on the tube surface with increasing q̇ due to the larger amount of liquid condensate formed per time period. The larger condensation rate at larger q̇ is also demonstrated in Fig. 6 with the help of two snapshots taken during the condensation experiments at two different q̇ of 6 and 20 kW·m− 2. Due to the experimental uncertainty of α cond, the small influence of ϑ sat on α cond at a given q̇ cannot be resolved.

The measured values from this work agree within uncertainties with the theoretical results based on Nußelt over the entire investigated q̇ range with an average absolute relative deviation (AARD) of 11 % with respect to the results from Nußelt's theory. Especially at q̇ above 6 kW·m− 2 , both results are always within 10 % and the AARD reduces to 6.5 %. At smaller q̇, the measured values from this work are consistently larger than the theoretical ones. Such an underprediction by Nußelt's theory has also been reported by other authors [6,7,31] where different interpretations have been given. One possible explanation is that, in contrast to the assumption in Nußelt's theory, the condensation process at small q̇ is not completely stationary, since droplets form on the bottom of the tube and drag liquid condensate with them when they are torn off, causing the condensate film to thin out considerably. Such a transient thinning of the liquid condensate film could also be observed visually during the measurements in this work. Furthermore, it was observed that the tubes slightly sagged in the center of the condenser, which causes liquid condensate at the bottom of the tube to be drawn towards the center. Also this behavior could promote thinning or of the liquid film at low q̇, as suggested by Gebauer et al. [7].

Crosses in Fig. 5 indicate the experimental data from Gebauer et al. [7] for the condensation of propane on smooth copper tubes at ϑ sat = 37 °C, which can be compared with the present data due to the small influence of ϑ sat. In the range between q̇ = 12 and 20 kW·m-2, the measured values agree well with those presented in this work. The data of Gebauer et al. [7] are systematically larger at q̇ < 12 kW·m− 2 and smaller at q̇ > 20 kW·m− 2 than those from this work. However, since the results are still well within combined uncertainties, the cause of this difference cannot be determined. Furthermore, the data obtained on the smooth tube presented in this paper were compared with the data of Jung et al. [32], who measured the condensation of propane on a smooth copper tube at ϑ sat = 39 °C. Since their results are solely given graphically and overlaid with further measurement data, only a qualitative comparison was possible. Taking the increased uncertainties through extracting the data from the figures in this publication into consideration, agreement within 20 % and, thus, within combined uncertainties, may be concluded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influences of fin density, fin height, and tube material
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the impact of a surface modification in the form of fins on α cond, the condensation of propane on copper tubes with fin densities of 19, 30, 35, 40, and 48 fpi and a fin height of 0.9 mm as well as a high-performance condensation tube (HPT) were investigated at ϑ sat = 30 and 40 °C. Similar as shown for the smooth Cu tube in the previous chapter, the influence of ϑ sat on α cond is very weak and cannot be resolved outside uncertainties. Therefore, the discussion of the influence of the fin density on α cond is based on measurements at ϑ sat = 40 °C. The results of the determined α cond as function of q̇ at ϑ sat = 40 °C are shown in Fig. 7 . Similar to the smooth tube, α cond for the finned tubes also decreases with increasing q̇ which can again be attributed to the increasing amount of liquid condensate on the tube surface and the corresponding increasing in the heat transfer resistance. Furthermore, α cond increases significantly with increasing fin density over the entire measured q̇ range. Park and Jung [33] studied the condensation heat transfer coefficient of propane on a low fin tube and a Turbo-C tube. Their data also showed a similar trend and a significant increase in α cond with the low fin tube compared to the smooth tube. The increase was even more pronounced for the Turbo-C tube. However, a quantitative comparison was not possible as the data was only published in graphical form.

Compared with the theoretical results based on Nußelt's film theory for the smooth Cu tube, which has approximately the same maximum diameter of 19 mm, α cond is increased by a factor of approximately 9 for the 19 fpi tube and by a factor of about 20 for the 48 fpi tube. In the case of the HPT, an even larger enhancement factor of approximately 28 could be observed. A part of these enhancements can be attributed to the increasing enlargement of surface area compared to the smooth tube with increasing fin density. To determine the impact of the surface enlargement to the increase in α cond, the ratio between the surface area of the finned tube and that of the smooth tube was calculated. This ratio is approximately 2.4 and 4.4 for the 19 and 48 fpi tube, respectively. Comparing the surface enlargement ratios with the actual enhancement factors of α cond, it becomes clear that a considerable portion of the improvements is attributed to another effect caused by the fin structure. This is most probably related to a reduction of the mean condensate film thickness caused by capillary forces leading to a drainage of the liquid condensate from the fin tips into the channels between the fins. This thins out the condensate film especially at the edges of the fin tips and reduces the condensate-related heat transfer resistance. Kleiner et al. [34] performed CFD simulations with pentane on various finned tubes. They were able to show that the mean film thickness on the fin flanks is up to 10 times smaller than the mean film thicknesses that form on the smooth tube according to Nußelt's theory even at relatively low q̇.

Furthermore, it can be seen from the measured data that the relative decrease in α cond with increasing q̇ is more pronounced for tubes with a small fin density. This suggests that for condensation tubes with a high fin density, the channels between the fins are already noticeably filled by liquid condensate at small q̇ and, therefore, an increase of q̇ affects the filling degree of the fin channels less than for tubes with larger distances between the fins. The overall highest α cond was determined for the HPT Cu tube. This agrees with the previous discussed observations and indicates that the elaborate surface modification for such high-performance condensation tubes further increases the surface area and favors the drainage of liquid condensate into the fin channels.

In a next step, the influence of the condensation tube material and the fin height on α cond was investigated. For this purpose, α cond of two 30 fpi tubes made of copper, Cu, or a copper-nickel alloy, CuNi, and two different fin heights was measured at ϑ sat = 40 °C. The results for α cond as function of q̇ for the four different condensation tubes are displayed in Fig. 8 .

The results show that α cond for the Cu tubes is always larger than for the CuNi tubes. For a fin height of 0.9 mm, α cond for the CuNi tube is approximately 65 % of the value for the Cu tube. Since the fin densities and the fin heights and geometries are comparable, the reason for this pronounced difference can be found in the lower thermal conductivity of the CuNi alloy, which is approximately λ CuNi = 50 W·m− 1·K− 1 and, thus, about 16 % of the value for the used Cu material of λ Cu = 310 W·m− 1·K− 1. Here it should be mentioned that, according to Eq. (4), α cond is calculated with respect to A out, i.e. the tube surface at the fin root. For this reason, the thermal resistance of the fin material is included in the calculation of the condensation heat transfer coefficient α cond. Due to the smaller λ CuNi, the T change along the fins is more pronounced for CuNi, which ultimately reduces the T difference between the fin tips and the saturated vapor and, therefore, explains the differences between the measured values of both tube materials. For both materials, the higher fins lead to larger α cond, which can be explained by the larger surface area provided by the longer fins and the deeper channels between the fins, that go along with a larger unflooded fin surface fraction. Comparing the differences resulting from the different fin height for the same tube material, it is noticeable that the increase due to the additional fin height is significantly less pronounced for CuNi. This observation can also be explained by the smaller λ CuNi in comparison to λ Cu. Since the thermal resistance increases with increasing fin height and decreasing λ, the positive effect of a larger surface area is counterbalanced by the larger additional conduction resistance in the CuNi fins in comparison with Cu fins.

To investigate whether the experimentally identified influences of the fin density, fin height, and tube material on α cond can also be determined with the help of the analytic model of Al-Badri et al. [6,16], the model was used to calculate α cond for the same finned condensation tubes summarized in Table 1 and being the basis for the results shown in Figs. 7 and 8. To evaluate the predictive capabilities of the model, the predicted heat transfer coefficients from the model α cond,pred are compared with the experimental data α cond,exp in Fig. 9 in the form of a parity plot. Since α cond from the analytical model is determined iteratively, q̇ does not match exactly to that of the experimental results. Therefore, α cond,exp was fitted with respect to q̇ using a 3rd order polynomial function.

The results in Fig. 9 show that even though the analytical model of Al-Badri et al. [6,16] tends to underpredict α cond, on average a good agreement between the experimental and predicted values can be found over the entire investigate range between 10 and 30 kW·m− 2·K− 1. The AARD and the bias for the predicted α cond from the experimental data are 15 % and −14 %, respectively. The maximum deviation was determined for the 19 fpi copper finned tube with a deviation of −37 % from the fit of the experimental results at ϑ sat = 40 °C and q̇ = 9.5 kW·m− 2. Considering the expanded experimental uncertainties for α cond between 5 and 15 %, a large part of α cond data are predicted within their uncertainties. Furthermore, it should be mentioned here that no systematic drift between the predicted and experimentally determined α cond is observable, but rather a constant shift of about 14 %. A closer look into the deviations shows that for all investigated condensation tubes, α cond at the higher investigated q̇ seems to be underpredicted most by the model. Since a considerable and systematic variation of the condensation tubes has been performed in this work, the question remains whether this offset is due to the thermophysical properties of the refrigerant propane. To answer this question, the model of Al-Badri et al. [6,16] should also be applied to a wider variety of refrigerants.

To investigate the influence of inundation on α cond in tube bundles, the condensation tubes are irrigated with liquid condensate at a fixed q̇. The corresponding mass flow is measured in the experiment and expressed with the help of the film Reynolds number Re film. The results from the inundation experiments are summarized in Table 3 .

Fig. 10 shows exemplarily the condensation behavior at q̇ = 20 kW·m− 2 for the 30 fpi Cu tube without irrigation and with an irrigation flow characterized by Re film = 1230. Here, it should be mentioned again that all inundation experiments were performed at the uppermost row of condensation tubes, since only here defined inundation conditions can be adjusted with the help of the perforated irrigation tubes. The influence of the inundation effect on α cond for copper condensation tubes with different fin densities and a fin height of 0.9 mm and a fixed value for q̇ of approximately 20 kW·m− 2 is shown in Fig. 11 as a function of Re film in form of the actual α cond related to the value of the first tube row α ref as given in Fig. 7. However, it must be mentioned at this point that, as already shown in Fig. 7, significantly higher α cond can be achieved by higher fin densities compared to smooth tubes.

The results in Fig. 11 show that the Re film-dependent trend of the inundation effect considerably varies for the different investigated tube types. In the case of the smooth tube, α cond initially decreases, reaches a minimum at Re film ≈ 800, and increases at larger Re film exceeding α ref for the single tube. Similar to the single tube measurements, the initial decrease can be associated with an increase in the condensate film thickness, which corresponds with visual observations. The subsequent increase may be explained by increasing turbulence and surface waviness with increasing inundation, which dominates over the thickness effect beyond Re film ≈ 2500. These observations agree well with previous investigations of the influence of inundation on α cond by Gebauer et al. [7] and Al-Badri et al. [35].

For the two finned condensation tubes, an initial increasing tendency of α cond with increasing Re film, yet within the measurement uncertainties, can be found. Such an initial increase could also be observed by other authors who studied integral finned tubes with the refrigerant R134a [36,37]. A possible explanation for this phenomenon is that condensate dripping on the condensation tubes leads to flow irregularities in the condensate film, which reduces the respective thermal resistance. After the initial increase, α cond continuously decreases with increasing Re film and reaches values more than 25 % lower than the single tube value in the case of the condensation tube with a fin density of 48 fpi. In this regime, it seems that the additional heat transfer resistance due to the increasing mean film thickness and flooding of the channels between the fins caused by the increasing mass flow of liquid condensate dominates over the aforementioned effects of flow irregularities. Comparing the finned tubes with different fin density, the offset of the data given in Fig. 11 is not significant since the values at a given Re film agree within their uncertainties. It seems, however, that the impact of inundation is more unfavorable for the tubes with larger fin density, which may be related to a more pronounced flooding of the narrower channels between the fins.

In the case of the high-performance condensation tube, a continuous decrease of α cond with increasing Re film can be observed. Here, it seems that due to the more intricate fin structure, the positive effect of flow irregularities in the condensate film cannot exceed the effect the increasing condensate film thickness including inundation of the fin channels. This leads to a more significant decrease of α cond with increasing Re film for the HPT in comparison to the finned tubes. It should be mentioned, however, that due to the distinctly larger α ref for the HPT, the HPT show larger α cond than the finned tubes even though α cond decreases more strongly with Re film.

Additional investigations of the inundation effect at q̇ = 60 kW·m− 2 have shown that the Re film-dependent trends of α cond ·α ref −1 are very similar for those conditions and are therefore not further discussed here.

The condensation heat transfer coefficient α cond of propane on single horizontal tubes and in tube bundles is investigated using a newly-designed experimental apparatus. The experimental setup is able to determine α cond with typical expanded experimental uncertainties (k = 2) in the range of 5 to 15 % and to characterize the influence of various condensation tube structures and materials as well as the inundation effect.

By investigating α cond for smooth copper tubes, the new experimental apparatus could be validated and good agreement of the data with theoretical values based on Nußelt's theory as well as experimental data from the literature could be found. For copper condensation tubes with varying fin densities, the increase in α cond by a factor of 9 to 20 in comparison to that for the smooth copper tube has been shown to be much larger than what can be expected from the increase in the surface area. One possible reason for the much larger α cond for the finned tubes might be the drainage of liquid condensate from the fin tips into the channels between the fins, which reduces the condensate film on the fin tips and increases α cond. The comparison between finned tubes based on copper and a copper-nickel alloy has shown that, in general, smaller α cond are found for the copper-nickel alloy due to the smaller thermal conductivity of the material. An increased fin height from 0.6 to 0.9 mm was found to considerably increase α cond for finned copper tubes, which can be explained by the increased surface area. This increase, however, is more pronounced for copper tubes than for copper-nickel tubes. The results suggest that when the fin height increases, the increase in the surface area is counterbalanced by increased thermal resistance for heat conduction in the fins. In the low-λ copper-nickel alloy, the thermal resistance in the fins increases more strongly with increasing fin height which leads to a weaker increase of α cond in comparison to the copper tubes.

A comparison between the experimental data and predicted values for α cond using the analytical model of Al-Badri et al. [6,16] has shown good agreement with an average absolute relative deviation of 15 % with respect to the experimental data from this work.

Finally, the investigation of the tube bundle or inundation effect has shown that α cond depends on the surface geometry and cannot be generalized. For smooth copper tubes, α cond was found to initially decrease before reaching a minimum and to further increase again with increasing amount of inundation. This can be explained by an initial increase in the condensate film thickness, followed by an improved heat transfer through the condensate film due to turbulences caused by the impinging condensate. For finned tubes, on the other hand, a weak initial increase in α cond with increasing amount of inundation, followed by a continuous decrease could be observed. Also here, the increase might be related to an improved heat transfer through the condensate film due to turbulences, while the pronounced decrease might be related to a flooding of the channels between the fins. The high-performance condensation tubes show the best overall α cond, although the inundation reduces α cond continuously with increasing inundation mass flow.
The present work has not been submitted elsewhere for publication, in whole or in part, and all the authors listed have approved the manuscript that is enclosed.

Supporting information: The primary measurement data from the experiments, namely the temperature, pressure, and volume flow rates, as well as the required data for accessing α cond on the basis of the primary measurement data are summarized.

Julius V.W. Kühl: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Visualization, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Julius H. Jander: Resources, Methodology. Maximilian Piszko: Resources, Methodology. Detlef Freitag: Resources. Jochen Dietl: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Jean El-Hajal: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Achim Gotterbarm: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Michael H. Rausch: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Tobias Klein: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Andreas P. Fröba: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization.

The authors gratefully acknowledge funding of the Erlangen Graduate School in Advanced Optical Technologies (SAOT) by the Bavarian State Ministry for Science and Art.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.J. Park, D. Jung, Condensation heat transfer coefficients of flammable refrigerants on various enhanced tubes, J. Mech. Sci. Technol., 19(10) (2005) 1957–1963.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detailed CFD simulations of pure substance condensation on horizontal annular low finned tubes including a parameter study of the fin slope

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Kleiner, A. Eder, S. Rehfeldt, H. Klein, Detailed CFD simulations of pure substance condensation on horizontal annular low finned tubes including a parameter study of the fin slope, Int. J. Heat Mass Transf., 163 (2020) 120363.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The influence of fin structure and fin density on the condensation heat transfer of R134a on single finned tubes and in tube bundles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.R. Al-Badri, A. Bär, A. Gotterbarm, M.H. Rausch, A.P. Fröba, The influence of fin structure and fin density on the condensation heat transfer of R134a on single finned tubes and in tube bundles, Int. J. Heat Mass Transf., 100 (2016) 582–589.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Influence of condensate inundation on heat transfer of R134a condensing on three dimensional enhanced tubes and integral-fin tubes with high fin density

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.T. Ji, C.Y. Zhao, D.C. Zhang, Y.L. He, W.Q. Tao, Influence of condensate inundation on heat transfer of R134a condensing on three dimensional enhanced tubes and integral-fin tubes with high fin density, Appl. Therm. Energy, 38 (2012) 151–159.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Film condensation of R-134a on tube arrays with plain and enhanced surfaces: part I–experimental heat transfer coefficients

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Gstoehl, J.R. Thome, Film condensation of R-134a on tube arrays with plain and enhanced surfaces: part I–experimental heat transfer coefficients, J. Heat Transf., 128(1) (2005) 21–32.
